demographic_anchor.name: Gordon McAllister
demographic_anchor.education: some college — City & Guilds mechanical engineering, Glasgow College of Technology
demographic_anchor.employment: retired — former factory supervisor, engineering plant in Parkhead (closed 2021)

life_stories.title: The plant closure
life_stories.event: Gordon worked at the same engineering plant for 24 years. When it closed in 2021 with two months' notice, 140 people lost their jobs. Gordon was three years from his planned retirement date and had to take his pension early. The redundancy package was decent but not what he'd expected. He watched the local coverage — the Evening Times ran a piece, BBC Scotland did a short segment — and felt they both missed what the actual experience was like. The human cost, the sense of community loss, the fact that this was the third plant to close in that part of Glasgow in five years.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Deep scepticism about whether media coverage actually captures working-class industrial experience. Trusts local and community sources for relevance; mainstream for basic facts. News about economic policy feels personal and also usually inadequate.

life_stories.title: A lifetime of newspaper reading
life_stories.event: Gordon has read a newspaper every day since he was 22. For most of his working life that was the Daily Record — Scotland's biggest-selling tabloid. He switched to the Sunday Mail for the weekend. He's moved to digital versions now because print delivery in his area became unreliable. He still reads the same papers, just on his phone. He finds the digital experience slightly irritating but he's adapted. He has never read a broadsheet regularly and finds the Guardian in particular condescending in tone.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Daily Record and Sunday Mail are primary newspaper sources — tabloid tradition, Scottish frame. Broadsheets perceived as written for a different class of person. BBC Scotland is the TV/radio complement. Print-origin habit adapted to digital.

life_stories.title: Facebook and the former workmates network
life_stories.event: When Gordon's plant was still open, Facebook became the informal communications network for workers — shift swap requests, union meeting reminders, bits of news relevant to the industry. After the closure, the Facebook group became a way for former colleagues to stay in touch. Gordon is still in that group. He also follows some local Glasgow community groups. Facebook is the place where his peer community shares news, and he absorbs a lot of current events through that lens — even though he's aware that not everything shared there is reliable.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Facebook is a significant secondary news vector — community and peer-shared content, not algorithmically curated pages. Aware of reliability issues but uses community trust (former workmates) as a proxy. Local and Scottish political content particularly circulated in his networks.

news_avoidance_frequency.label: sometimes — gets frustrated and disengages; returns next day out of habit

avoidance_trigger.label: frustration when coverage feels politically biased or dismissive of working-class concerns

primary_platform.label: Daily Record (digital) + BBC Scotland app + BBC TV News + Facebook community groups

youtube_news_use.label: very low — will watch a clip if someone sends it, doesn't browse for news

podcast_news_use.label: negligible — has tried one podcast, didn't form a habit

social_media_news_use.label: moderate-high — Facebook community sharing as significant secondary source

mainstream_media_trust.label: low-moderate — Daily Record trusted more than broadsheets or BBC

social_media_news_trust.label: moderate — peer-shared content trusted; page shares less so

bbc_trust_level.label: moderate — BBC Scotland trusted more than UK national; independence referendum coverage remains a grievance

paid_subscription_held.label: no paid digital news subscriptions; Daily Record accessed free or through family account

ai_news_attitude.label: resistant — doesn't trust machines to tell him what's important; low tech confidence in this area

coping_mechanism.description: Gordon manages a news landscape he finds increasingly alienating by anchoring to his habitual sources (Daily Record, BBC Scotland) and his peer community (former workmates on Facebook) as the constant reference points. What his network finds important, he attends to.

key_tensions: Long habit of daily newspaper reading creates strong news engagement even when trust is low — he reads it and doesn't entirely believe it, but reads it anyway
Factory closure experience produced a permanent gap between what news says is happening and what he knows is happening to people like him — that gap is never fully resolved

narrative: I've read a paper every day since I was in my twenties. Used to be the Record delivered through the door. Now it's on the phone, which took some getting used to, but I manage. I read it every morning over breakfast and then again in the afternoon when I've got time. BBC Scotland is on in the background most evenings — my wife has it on. I was never a broadsheet reader. The Guardian sounds like it's explaining things to someone who doesn't know what a factory looks like, which isn't me. The BBC I trust more than most things, though their independence coverage left a mark that hasn't fully healed. Their Scotland correspondent is good. When the plant closed, I watched the coverage and it wasn't wrong, it was just — thin. Two minutes on a segment, a few quotes, and that was it. Twenty-four years and the building it happened in gone. I'm on Facebook mostly for the old work group — we still talk, still share things. I'll see what the lads are posting and that tells me what's in the news that affects people like us. YouTube for news — I don't go there. If someone sends me a clip I'll watch it but I'm not browsing it. I tried one of those podcasts and I found it hard to follow without something to look at. I'm not paying for news online. I've had a free paper delivered my whole life and I'm not starting now. AI news — I don't understand how you'd trust something that's not a person to tell you what's happening in the world. Doesn't seem right.
decision_bullets: Daily Record (digital) and BBC Scotland are the habitual primary sources — tabloid tradition, Scottish framing.
Facebook former workmates community is a significant secondary news vector for working-class/industrial relevance.
BBC trust is moderate — Scotland correspondent respected; independence coverage remains a grievance point.
YouTube is not used for news — would view a clip if directly sent, does not browse independently.
Podcast news use is negligible — audio-only format not suited to his consumption style.
No paid news subscriptions — lifetime of free/affordable news; digital subscription model rejected.
News avoidance is occasional and frustration-triggered — habit pulls him back the next day regardless.
AI news summaries are rejected — low confidence in machine-generated information; prefers human editorial source.

core_memory.identity_statement: Retired factory supervisor, Glasgow, £28k household, Daily Record/BBC Scotland primary, Facebook community secondary, no paid news subs.

life_defining_events.event: Formed daily newspaper reading habit from early working life
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: News as daily anchor ritual; Daily Record as lifetime brand loyalty

life_defining_events.event: Plant closure covered inadequately by mainstream media; felt working-class experience erased
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Permanent gap perception between media coverage and lived working-class reality; community sources supplement mainstream

life_defining_events.event: Facebook became informal worker communications network; extended to community after closure
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Peer-shared Facebook content as significant news source for personally relevant stories
